name: Pleiades
description: The Pleiades were the seven daughters of the Titan Atlas and the Oceanid Pleione. Their names were Alcyone, Asterope, Celaeno, Electra, Maia, Merope, and Taygete. They were nymphs in the retinue of Artemis, known for their beauty and grace. Their story varies depending on the source, but they are often pursued by various gods and heroes. Some accounts claim they were transformed into stars to escape the relentless pursuit of Orion, the giant hunter. Others say they committed suicide out of grief for their sisters, the Hyades, or for their father, Atlas, who was forced to hold up the sky. Regardless of the reason, Zeus immortalized them as a constellation, a cluster of stars that can be seen in the night sky. In some variations, only six stars are visible, with Merope hiding her face in shame for being the only one to marry a mortal. The Pleiades represent beauty, vulnerability, and the power of familial bonds. They are a reminder of the constant interplay between the mortal and immortal realms.
subtitle: Seven sisters turned into stars
appearance: The Pleiades are described as exceptionally beautiful nymphs, often depicted with radiant complexions and flowing hair. Their attire is typically light and airy, befitting their connection to nature and the heavens. As stars, they appear as a close-knit cluster of shimmering lights in the night sky, a recognizable constellation easily visible to the naked eye in clear conditions. Each star represents one of the sisters, though only six are commonly visible.
habitat: Originally, they inhabited Mount Cyllene in Arcadia, Greece, and were associated with forests, meadows, and springs, typical habitats for nymphs. After their transformation, their habitat became the night sky, where they reside as the Pleiades constellation.
behavior: As nymphs, the Pleiades were playful and enjoyed dancing and hunting with Artemis. They were also known for their gentle nature and familial devotion. Their transformation into stars reflects their enduring bond and their escape from earthly troubles. Their presence in the sky serves as a constant reminder of their story and a source of guidance for sailors and travelers.
weaknesses: Dependence on each other (separation weakens them)

keyMyths.title: Orion's Pursuit
keyMyths.description: Orion relentlessly pursued the Pleiades for years. To protect them from his advances, Zeus transformed them into doves and then into stars, placing them in the sky as the Pleiades constellation. This chase is said to continue eternally across the heavens.

keyMyths.title: Atlas's Burden
keyMyths.description: As daughters of Atlas, who was forced to hold up the sky, the Pleiades shared in their father's suffering. Some accounts claim they were driven to grief by his plight, leading to their transformation into stars as a form of escape and remembrance. This grief is sometimes associated with the dimming of one of the stars.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Early Greek poet who mentions the Pleiades and their parentage.
sources.text: Early Greek poet who mentions the Pleiades and their parentage.

sources.author: Hyginus
sources.description: Roman writer who provides detailed accounts of the Pleiades and their stories.
sources.text: Roman writer who provides detailed accounts of the Pleiades and their stories.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: Roman poet who recounts the legends of the Pleiades in his calendar of Roman festivals.
